headline: 8 contracts catalogued from Etherscan on Ethereum; bridge infrastructure, gateways, and inbox identified

findings.text: Etherscan name-tag search on Ethereum surfaced 8 addresses: main Bridge (0x8315177a...), Old Bridge (0x011b6e24...), L1 Gateway Router (0x72ce9c84...), L1 ERC20 Gateway (0xa3a7b6f8...), L1 DAI Gateway (0xD3B5b600...), Delayed Inbox (0x4dbd4fc5...), Bridge Utils (0x84eFa170...), Bridge 2 (0x2f06e43D...)

Official deployments doc identified at https://docs.arbitrum.io/build-decentralized-apps/reference/contract-addresses but direct fetch failed; page metadata indicates it lists protocol, token bridge, and precompile contract addresses
No audit links recorded in per-protocol context; none fetched
GitHub repository OffchainLabs/token-bridge-contracts identified as canonical source in official documentation; direct fetch failed, requires content-addressed commit SHA for future runs
Single chain (Ethereum) specified in protocol.chains; cross-chain comparison not applicable
Factory contract enumeration deferred: L1GatewayRouter identified as router but no factory pattern enumeration method calls completed this run
Addresses catalogued with preliminary role assignments: Bridge (main), router, gateway, inbox, and utility contracts identified
All 8 admin_addresses entries trace to Etherscan search results

rationale.verdict: Discovery slice: Walked Etherscan name-tag search for Ethereum, identified 8 core bridge/gateway/inbox/router contracts with role-based taxonomy. Official deployments doc referenced but not fetched. GitHub repo identified but SHA not pinned. No audits recorded. Deferred factory enumeration and proxy admin extraction to downstream slices.

evidence.shows: Arbitrum Bridge (main) on Ethereum named 'Arbitrum: Bridge' in Etherscan tags
Legacy Arbitrum bridge named 'Arbitrum: Old Bridge' on Ethereum
L1GatewayRouter proxy at 0x72ce9c84... with implementation at 0x52595021fa01b3e14ec6c88953afc8e35dff423c, EIP-1967 Transparent Proxy pattern
Arbitrum One: L1 ERC20 Gateway named 'Arbitrum One · Nitro · Bridge' on Etherscan, holds >$1.2B in ERC-20 tokens
Arbitrum One: L1 DAI Gateway on Ethereum using L1CrossDomainEnabled pattern
Arbitrum Delayed Inbox address 0x4dbd4fc535ac27206064b68ffcf827b0a60bab3f confirmed as the contract receiving user deposits to the bridge
Arbitrum: Bridge Utils utility contract on Ethereum, implements BridgeUtils for getCountsAndAccumulators method
Arbitrum: Bridge 2 contract tagged on Etherscan, appears to be historical or secondary bridge variant
Official Arbitrum docs page listing deployed smart contract addresses across chains, including protocol, token bridge, and precompile contracts
OffchainLabs canonical token bridge contracts repository; documentation states all public Arbitrum chains include a canonical token bridge deployment with Gateway and Gateway Router contracts

Arbitrum canonical bridge architecture: L1GatewayRouter initiates Ethereum-to-Arbitrum transfers, forwards to appropriate gateway (L1ERC20Gateway, L1ArbitrumGateway, etc), communicates via retryable tickets to L2 counterparts

unknowns: D2: official deployments doc at docs.arbitrum.io/build-decentralized-apps/reference/contract-addresses could not be fetched; page structure and complete address list remain unverified
D4: GitHub OffchainLabs/token-bridge-contracts repository identified but not fetched; deployment artifacts (broadcast/, deployments/) not enumerated; no commit SHA pinned
D6: Factory enumeration deferred; L1GatewayRouter likely enumerates gateways via view methods (getGateway, allGateways, etc.) but calls not attempted; cap at 50 would apply if enumerated
D8: Proxy admin and implementation addresses for L1GatewayRouter (proxy at 0x72ce9c84..., impl at 0x52595021...) identified in Etherscan but not verified via read-API; admin actor class unknown
D8-offchain: L1GatewayRouter and gateway contracts' owner/admin addresses not fetched via read-API due to fetch tool exhaustion; downstream slices required

protocol_metadata.about: Arbitrum's canonical token bridge facilitates cross-chain asset transfers between Ethereum (L1) and Arbitrum (L2) using a gateway architecture implemented by Offchain Labs. The bridge deploys paired gateway contracts on both chains that manage ERC-20 token deposits, withdrawals, and custody, communicating via retryable tickets. Users interact with the L1GatewayRouter to initiate transfers, which routes to the appropriate gateway contract for escrow and cross-chain messaging.
